portal_system: Florida Lobbyist Registration & Compensation Reporting System (LRCRS, floridalobbyist.gov) — single unified portal that internally separates Legislative-branch vs Executive-branch registration/compensation, with two distinct regulators behind it
regulator: Florida Joint Legislative Auditing Committee (legislative) + Florida Commission on Ethics (executive); Lobbyist Registration Office (111 W. Madison St., Tallahassee) operates the shared portal on behalf of both

seed_urls.notes: LRCRS landing page; explicitly states 'ONE portal for BOTH lobbyist registrations and compensation reports' covering both legislative and executive regimes. Live counts shown: Legislative ~1,705 lobbyists / 4,797 principals / 14,261 registrations; Executive ~1,337 lobbyists / 4,596 principals / 13,307 registrations.

'Other Resources' hub page; canonical index of governing laws (legislative §§11.044–11.062, §350.031; executive §§112.3215–112.3217 + FAC Ch. 34-12), data downloads, FAQs, application user guide, deadlines (Q1 May 15 / Q2 Aug 14 / Q3 Nov 14 / Q4 Feb 14).
Unified lobbyist search; users select Legislative vs Executive branch and search by Name / Location / Principal / Industry. Covers BOTH regimes through one UI.

Legislative regime — Registered Legislative Branch Lobbyist Directory (PDFs by lobbyist name and by principal name, 2006–2026). This is the public-facing registry view, not the filer login. Includes special CRC 2017–2018 registrations.

Executive regime — Registered Executive Branch Lobbyist Directory (PDFs by lobbyist name and by principal name, 2006–2026). Same shape as the legislative directory but distinct dataset administered by Commission on Ethics.

Compensation Report Search — quarterly compensation reports searchable by Firm / Lobbyist / Principal, filterable by reporting quarter (Q1–Q4), calendar year (2007–2027), and Branch (Legislative or Executive). Serves both regimes from the same UI.

Online Sunshine bulk-download index; serves two tab-delimited (.TXT) files — Legislative Lobbyist File and Executive Lobbyist File — both updated daily per page text. Page also exposes a 'File Definition Description' PDF (data dictionary) link from this index. Lobbyist/principal/firm fields per file.

Legislative regime — 28-question FAQ for Florida Legislature lobbyists; covers registration mechanics, principal authorization, fees, compensation reporting, deadlines, contingency-fee prohibition. Authoritative interpretive guidance for the legislative side.

Executive regime — 28-question FAQ for Executive Branch lobbyists; covers registration, $25/principal annual fee, renewal cycles, quarterly reporting (45-day deadline), subcontracting, mixed legislative/executive lobbying allocation.

Legislative regime — Online Sunshine archive of legislative-branch quarterly compensation reports (PDFs grouped by quarter and alphabetical bucket). Complementary to the LRCRS search; useful for older quarters and bulk PDF pulls.

Legislative regime statute — F.S. §11.045 'Lobbying before the Legislature; registration and reporting; exemptions; penalties' on Online Sunshine (Title III, Chapter 11). Authoritative state-source URL; the broader §§11.044–11.062 cluster also governs.

Executive regime statute — F.S. §112.3215 'Lobbying before the executive branch or the Constitution Revision Commission; registration and reporting; investigation by commission' on Online Sunshine (Title X, Chapter 112, Part III). Companion §§112.3216–112.3217 also apply.

Legislative regime regulator — Joint Legislative Auditing Committee (JLAC) about page. JLAC's lobbying nexus is narrower than 'register the lobbyists': it adopts audit guidelines, maintains the approved-auditor list, and runs random selection for audits of compensation reports filed by both legislative and executive branch firms.

Executive regime regulator — Florida Commission on Ethics 'About Us'. Nine-member independent commission (5 Gov / 2 Sen Pres / 2 House Spkr appointees, 2-year terms), staff ~25 in Tallahassee. Administers Executive Branch Lobbyist Registration System; investigates breaches of public trust.

Executive regime — Commission on Ethics 'Lobbyist Information' page; bridges ethics.state.fl.us users to floridalobbyist.gov for registration/reporting and surfaces statutory framework (contingency-fee prohibition, public-funds restrictions), FAQ links, complaint forms, advisory opinions.

flagged.notes: The bulk-download index page (verified seed above) advertises a 'File Definition Description' PDF link, but the inferred path returns 404 — actual filename differs. Stage 2 should scrape the dl_data.cfm page to extract the live PDF href rather than hard-coding it.

The 'Other Resources' page (verified seed above) references an 'Application User Guide (PDF)' download but the exact href was not surfaced via WebFetch summarization. Stage 2 should scrape the LobbyistInformation page to extract the canonical PDF URL.

observations: Florida runs a deceptively simple-looking single portal (floridalobbyist.gov / LRCRS) that masks a genuine bifurcated regime: the Joint Legislative Auditing Committee owns legislative-branch lobbying oversight (statutes §§11.044–11.062) while the Florida Commission on Ethics administers executive-branch lobbying (statutes §§112.3215–112.3217 + FAC Ch. 34-12). The portal's search, registration, and compensation surfaces all expose a Branch=Legislative|Executive selector, so most disclosure UIs serve both regimes through one widget — but downstream parsing must keep the regime tag, since fees ($25/principal executive vs separate legislative fee schedule) and audit pipelines diverge. A separate Online Sunshine bulk-download index (leg.state.fl.us) hosts two tab-delimited registration files (one per regime, advertised as daily-refreshed) plus a File Definition Description PDF; this is the only true bulk channel — the rest of the portal is PDF-per-quarter or interactive search. Compared with the 2026-04-13 baseline (12 seeds, 12 snapshots, 0.3 MB), this rebuild yields 15 verified seeds, deliberately broader to cover both regulators' about pages and both statutes; small payload size in the original is consistent with Florida's heavy reliance on PDF reports rather than HTML/JSON endpoints.
